lineage: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
The Macedonians and Acacians meet at Antioch, and proclaim their Assent to the Nicene Creed.

title: The Macedonians and Acacians meet at Antioch, and proclaim their Assent to the Nicene Creed.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Meanwhile the state of the church was by no means tranquil; for the heads of the sects assiduously paid their court to the emperor their king that protection for themselves meant also power against their acknowledged opponents.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And first the Macedonians presented a petition to him, in which they begged that all those who asserted the Son to be unlike the Father, might be expelled from the churches, and themselves allowed to take their place.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This supplication was presented by Basil bishop of Ancyra, Silvanus of Tarsus, Sophronius of Pompeiopolis, Pasinicus of Zelæ, Leontius of Comana, Callicrates of Claudiopolis, and Theophilus of Castabala.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The emperor having perused it, dismissed them without any other answer than this: ‘I abominate contentiousness; but I love and honor those who exert themselves to promote unanimity.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: When this remark became generally known, it subdued the violence of those who were desirous of altercation and thus was realized in the design of the emperor.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: At this time the real spirit of the Acacian sect, and their readiness to accommodate their opinions to those invested with supreme authority, became more conspicuous than ever.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For assembling themselves at Antioch in Syria, they entered into a conference with Melitius, who had separated from them a little before, and embraced the ‘homoousian’ opinion.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This they did because they saw Melitius was in high estimation with the emperor, who then resided at Antioch; and assenting therefore by common consent, they drew up a declaration of their sentiments acknowledging the homoousion and ratifying the Nicene Creed and presented it to the emperor.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It was expressed in the following terms.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: ‘The Synod of bishops convened at Antioch out of various provinces, to the most pious and beloved of God, our lord Jovian Victor Augustus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: ‘That your piety has above all things aimed at establishing the peace and harmony of the church, we ourselves, most devout emperor, are fully aware.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Nor are we insensible that you have wisely judged an acknowledgment of the orthodox faith to be the sum and substance of this unity.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Wherefore lest we should be included in the number of those who adulterate the doctrine of the truth, we hereby declare to your piety that we embrace and steadfastly hold the faith of the holy Synod formerly convened at Nicæa.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Especially since the term homoousios, which to some seems novel and inappropriate, has been judiciously explained by the fathers to denote that the Son was begotten of the Father’s substance, and that he is like the Father as to substance.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Not indeed that any passion is to be understood in relation to that ineffable generation.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Nor is the term ousia, “substance,” taken by the fathers in any usual signification of it among the Greeks; but it has been employed for the subversion of what Arius impiously dared to assert concerning Christ, viz.—that he was made of things “not existing.”

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Which heresy the Anomœans, who have lately sprung up, still more audaciously maintain, to the utter destruction of ecclesiastical unity.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: We have therefore annexed to this our declaration, a copy of the faith set forth by the bishops assembled at Nicæa, with which also we are fully satisfied.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It is this: “We believe in one God the Father Almighty,” and all the rest of the Creed in full.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: We, the undersigned, in presenting this statement, most cordially assent to its contents.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Melitius bishop of Antioch, Eusebius of Samosata, Evagrius of Sicily, Uranius of Apamæa, Zoilus of Larissa, Acacius of Cæsarea, Antipater of Rhosus, Abramius of Urimi, Aristonicus of Seleucia-upon-Belus, Barlamenus of Pergamus, Uranius of Melitina, Magnus of Chalcedon, Eutychius of Eleutheropolis, Isacocis of Armenia Major, Titus of Bostra, Peter of Sippi, Pelagius of Laodicæa, Arabian of Antros, Piso of Adana through Lamydrion a presbyter, Sabinian bishop of Zeugma, Athanasius of Ancyra through Orphitus and Aëtius presbyters, Irenion bishop of Gaza, Piso of Augusta, Patricius of Paltus through Lamyrion a presbyter, Anatolius bishop of Berœa, Theotimus of the Arabs, and Lucian of Arca.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This declaration we found recorded in that work of Sabinus, entitled A Collection of the Acts of Synods.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Now the emperor had resolved to allay if possible the contentious spirit of the parties at variance, by bland manners and persuasive language toward them all; declaring that he ‘would not molest any one on account of his religious sentiments, and that he should love and highly esteem such as would zealously promote the unity of the church.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The philosopher Themistius attests that such was his conduct, in the oration he composed on his ‘consulate.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For he extols the emperor for his overcoming the wiles of flatterers by freely permitting every one to worship God according to the dictates of his conscience.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And in allusion to the check which the sycophants received, he facetiously observes that experience has made it evident that such persons ‘worship the purple and not God; and resemble the changeful Euripus, which sometimes rolls its waves in one direction, and at others the very opposite way.’
